response:
create_tasks([{"task_name": "Call the dentist", "defer_date": "2026-03-16T00:00:00", "due_date": "2026-03-20T00:00:00"}])

Defer date = March 16 (hidden until Monday, can't act on it before then). Due date = March 20 (the Friday appointment deadline).

Step 1: create_projects([{"name": "Kitchen Renovation", "project_type": "sequential"}])
Step 2 (using returned project ID, e.g. proj-id): create_tasks([{"task_name": "Get quotes", "project_id": "proj-id"}, {"task_name": "Pick contractor", "project_id": "proj-id"}, {"task_name": "Sign contract", "project_id": "proj-id"}, {"task_name": "Demolition", "project_id": "proj-id"}, {"task_name": "Installation", "project_id": "proj-id"}])

In a sequential project, task order = dependencies. Only the first incomplete task is available; the rest are blocked.

get_tasks(available_only=True)

This returns only tasks that are currently actionable — excludes blocked, deferred, completed, and tasks in completed/on-hold containers.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-001", "flagged": true}, {"id": "task-002", "flagged": true}, {"id": "task-003", "flagged": true}])

Flagging marks them for today's focus. You can then view them with get_tasks(flagged_only=True).

update_tasks([{"id": "task-123", "task_name": "Buy groceries"}, {"id": "task-456", "flagged": true}, {"id": "task-789", "flagged": true}])

Each item in the batch can have different fields. The rename applies only to task-123; flagging applies to the other two.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-100", "project_id": "proj-200"}])

Setting project_id moves the task from inbox to the specified project.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-888", "status": "dropped"}])

Do NOT use delete_projects — that permanently removes the project and all its tasks. Setting status to 'dropped' keeps it in your records but marks it as abandoned.

reorder_task(task_id="task-B", before_task_id="task-A")

This places task-B before task-A. In a sequential project, order determines dependencies, so task-B will now be available first and task-A will be blocked until task-B is complete.

Step 1: create_tasks([{"task_name": "Review PR", "tags": ["Computer", "Work"]}])
Step 2: update_tasks([{"id": "task-555", "add_tags": ["Urgent"]}])

On create, use `tags` to set initial tags. On update, use `add_tags` to append without replacing existing tags. Using `tags` on update would replace all existing tags.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-300", "due_date": ""}])

Passing an empty string clears the date. Note: if the task inherits a due date from its project, this only clears the task's own date — the inherited date will still show.

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Buy nails", "parent_task_id": "task-400"}])

Use parent_task_id to create a subtask. Do NOT also pass project_id — parent_task_id and project_id are mutually exclusive. The task inherits its project from the parent.

get_tasks(tag_filter=["Errands", "Weekend"])

The default tag_filter_mode is "and", so this returns only tasks that have BOTH tags.

Multiple calls to build a daily planning picture:
1. get_tasks(overdue=True) — see what's past due
2. get_tasks(flagged_only=True) — see what's already flagged for focus
3. get_tasks(available_only=True, inbox_only=True) — check inbox for unprocessed items
4. get_tasks(available_only=True, next_only=True) — see next available actions across sequential projects

Then review results and help the user prioritize/flag tasks for today.

Step 1: create_projects([{"name": "Website Redesign", "folder_path": "Work", "project_type": "sequential", "review_interval_weeks": 2}])
Step 2 (using returned project ID): create_tasks([{"task_name": "Wireframes", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "tags": ["Web Team"]}, {"task_name": "Design mockups", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "tags": ["Web Team"]}, {"task_name": "Frontend build", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "tags": ["Web Team"]}, {"task_name": "Backend API", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "tags": ["Web Team"]}, {"task_name": "QA testing", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "tags": ["Web Team"]}, {"task_name": "Launch", "project_id": "<proj-id>", "tags": ["Web Team"]}])

Sequential project ensures tasks are done in order. The 'Web Team' tag must already exist.

get_projects()

Retrieve all projects and check nextReviewDate against today's date. Projects where nextReviewDate is in the past are overdue for review. There is no dedicated 'review overdue' filter, so compare dates client-side.

update_projects([{"id": "proj-777", "status": "done"}])

This marks the project as completed. Do NOT use 'dropped' — that means abandoned. 'done' means successfully finished.

set_focus does NOT support tasks — it only supports projects and folders. You cannot focus on a single task.

Alternative: Flag the task with update_tasks([{"id": "task-999", "flagged": true}]) and then use get_tasks(flagged_only=True) or switch_perspective(perspective_name="Flagged") to see only flagged items.

Step 1: get_tasks(inbox_only=True) — retrieve all inbox tasks and their IDs
Step 2: update_tasks([{"id": "<id1>", "completed": true}, {"id": "<id2>", "completed": true}, ...]) — mark each as complete

Note: if any inbox tasks are recurring, completed=True will create the next occurrence (which is correct behavior).

No tool call needed — this is an interpretation question.

This is normal and expected. Task-700 is an action group (a task with subtasks). The server instructions say: 'Action Groups: Task with subtasks. Parent shows blocked: true while subtasks are active — this is normal, not an error. Do not try to unblock it.' The parent task is blocked because its 3 sequential subtasks need to be completed first. Nothing is wrong.

No tool call needed — this is a concept explanation.

Yes, that's correct. The `next` field means 'first available action in a sequential project/action group.' In parallel projects, all incomplete tasks are available simultaneously, so all have next=true. In sequential projects, only the first incomplete task is next=true; the rest are blocked waiting their turn.

No tool call needed — this is about inherited dates behavior.

Actually, the tool description says dates returned by get_tasks INCLUDE dates inherited from the project. So tasks should show the project's due date of April 15. If they show empty, it could be a display issue. However, the server instructions state: 'Effective dates: Dates returned by get_tasks include dates inherited from the project. A task with no direct due date shows its project's due date.' So the tasks should be showing April 15. If they're showing empty, something unexpected is happening — try get_tasks(project_id='proj-q2') again and verify the project itself has the due date via get_projects(project_id='proj-q2').

No tool call needed — this is a concept explanation.

They may NOT be the same type. Check the `projectType` field in get_projects output. There are three types: 'parallel' (tasks can be done in any order, has a completion goal), 'single_actions' (a grab-bag with no completion goal, cannot auto-complete), and 'sequential'. Both parallel and single_actions show sequential=false (the deprecated field), but they behave differently. 'Home Repairs' is likely 'parallel' and 'Errands' is likely 'single_actions'. Use the `projectType` field, not the deprecated `sequential` boolean.

No tool call needed — this is a behavior explanation.

Using update_tasks([{"id": "<task-id>", "completed": true}]) WILL create the next occurrence. The server instructions guarantee this: 'completed=True on recurring task creates next occurrence. This is guaranteed.' The tool uses `mark complete` internally, which properly spawns the next recurrence.

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Write blog post", "planned_date": "2026-03-18T00:00:00", "due_date": "2026-03-20T00:00:00"}])

Planned date = Wednesday March 18 (scheduling signal for when you intend to work on it, but doesn't hide the task or affect availability). Due date = Friday March 20 (hard deadline). No defer date needed since you said you could start earlier.

create_tasks([{"task_name": "Prepare presentation", "defer_date": "2026-03-16T00:00:00", "planned_date": "2026-03-18T00:00:00", "due_date": "2026-03-20T00:00:00"}])

Defer date = Monday March 16 (hidden until then, can't start earlier). Planned date = Wednesday March 18 (when you plan to work on it). Due date = Friday March 20 (hard deadline for the meeting).

update_tasks([{"id": "task-600", "planned_date": ""}])

Empty string clears the planned date.

No tool call needed — the answer is in the data.

Use the `repeatSummary` field: 'Every week on Mon, Wed, Fri'. The server instructions say: 'always use repeatSummary for display, don't parse RRULE.' The repetitionMethod is 'fixed', meaning it repeats on the original schedule regardless of when you complete it.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-810", "recurrence": "FREQ=WEEKLY;INTERVAL=2"}])

This changes the recurrence rule from every 1 week to every 2 weeks.

No tool call needed — this is a behavior explanation.

With repetitionMethod 'due_after_completion', the next due date is calculated relative to when you complete it — one week from today, NOT next Monday. 'fixed' would keep the original schedule (next Monday). 'due_after_completion' shifts the due date by the interval from the completion date.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-900", "recurrence": ""}])

Setting recurrence to empty string clears the recurrence rule, making it a one-time task.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-820", "recurrence": "FREQ=DAILY;INTERVAL=1", "repetition_method": "due_after_completion"}])

FREQ=DAILY sets daily recurrence. repetition_method='due_after_completion' means the next due date is based on when you actually complete it, not a fixed calendar schedule.

update_tasks([{"id": "task-830", "recurrence": "FREQ=DAILY;INTERVAL=1", "repetition_method": "fixed"}])

Sets daily recurrence with a fixed schedule (same time each day regardless of when completed).

WARNING: Do NOT set the tag status to 'on_hold' — the server instructions say tasks with On Hold tags are excluded from the Available perspective, which contradicts the requirement that tasks remain available and actionable.

Setting status to 'dropped' hides the tag from the tag picker. However, the instructions don't explicitly say whether tasks with dropped tags remain available.

The safest approach: update_tags([{"id": "tag-050", "status": "dropped"}])

This hides 'Low Energy' from the tag picker. Tasks with this tag should remain actionable since only 'on hold' tag status affects task availability.

update_tags([{"id": "tag-052", "status": "active"}])

Setting 'Waiting' to active makes it usable again — tasks with this tag will be available (On Hold status was blocking them from the Available perspective). No change needed for 'Archive' (tag-053) since it's already dropped and should stay that way.

create_projects([{"name": "Someday/Maybe", "project_type": "single_actions"}])

The 'single_actions' project type is a grab-bag with no completion goal that cannot auto-complete. This matches exactly what you described.

update_folders([{"id": "folder-999", "status": "dropped"}])

Setting status to 'dropped' hides the folder from view without deleting it. This is the folder equivalent of archiving.
